headline: Penang Semiconductor Cluster Faces Water Shortage Crisis
context: Penang's semiconductor manufacturing hub — producing 8% of global chip packaging output — faces recurring water supply disruptions as dam reserves fell below 50% in February 2026, threatening RM62 billion in annual production.

cards.big: RM62 billion semiconductor hub at risk — Penang's dams at 48% capacity while Intel and AMD demand 24/7 water supply
cards.sub: One day of water disruption costs Penang's chip industry RM170 million in output and global supply chain delays

cards.big: Penang's water demand at 1.1 billion litres daily exceeds sustainable supply of 980 million litres
cards.sub: Industrial water consumption grew 45% in 5 years from data centres and fabs — infrastructure investment grew only 12%

cards.big: Domestic water tariff in Penang at RM0.22 per 1,000 litres is cheapest in Malaysia — discouraging conservation
cards.sub: Average household wastes 38% of water through leaks and overconsumption enabled by artificially low pricing

cards.big: Penang Water Authority proposed RM1.2 billion dam expansion in 2019 — still awaiting federal EIA approval 7 years later
cards.sub: State-federal jurisdiction split on water means Penang cannot build infrastructure without federal environmental clearance

cards.big: Malaysia attracted RM62 billion in chip investment but can't supply the water to keep the factories running — that's strategic negligence
Should semiconductor-critical states like Penang receive emergency federal infrastructure funding bypassing normal approval timelines?
